failure_modes.text: Teams can treat verbal toughness as leverage even when the walk-away is not credible and the fallback option is worse than the current deal.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when a team treats verbal toughness as leverage even though the walk-away is not credible and the fallback option is worse than the current deal.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit walk-away credibility: before claiming leverage, verify that your fallback option is real, funded, and executable — not just a negotiating bluff.

failure_modes.text: A signed deal can be mistaken for durable control even though the next commitment hands timing, data, or switching-cost leverage to the counterparty.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when leaders mistake a signed contract for durable control even though the next operational commitment hands timing, data, or switching cost leverage to the counterparty.
failure_modes.mitigation: Track commitment gradients across the deal lifecycle: map where timing, data access, or switching costs shift leverage after signing, and negotiate protections for those inflection points.

failure_modes.text: Teams can win headline concessions while quietly giving away the commitment gradient that later inverts bargaining power.
failure_modes.source_quote: negotiating headline price while ignoring the commitment gradient
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish headline wins from structural control: after any negotiation, check whether the concessions you won matter less than the commitment dynamics you gave away.

premortem_questions.text: Which party can actually absorb a walk-away with less damage this quarter, and what evidence proves it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which party can absorb a walk-away with less damage this quarter, and what evidence proves it?

premortem_questions.text: What would make our walk-away or delay threat binding rather than rhetorical?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would make our walk-away or delay threat actually binding rather than rhetorical?

premortem_questions.text: At what milestone does our cost to reverse become higher than the counterparty's cost to lose us?
premortem_questions.source_quote: At what milestone does our cost to reverse become higher than the counterparty's cost to lose us?

heuristics.text: Map outside options before discussing terms because fallback quality is part of the real price floor.
heuristics.source_quote: Map outside options before discussing terms

heuristics.text: Separate stated willingness from credible walk-away by checking enforcement cost, internal approval, and operational fallback.
heuristics.source_quote: Separate stated willingness from credible walk-away

heuristics.text: Track leverage inversion over time so early wins do not hide later lock-in and dependency costs.

curation_notes.summary: The source is naturally chunk-ready on outside options, credible walk-away, commitment gradients, and post-signature leverage inversion.
donor_drops: Did not preserve generic negotiation playbooks because the raw source is stronger on leverage diagnostics and hidden dependency structure.
open_questions: The third failure mode compresses several commitment-gradient passages so the chunk remains concise.

allies: The raw ally surface is strongest on incentives, lock-in, principal-agent-problem, and optionality because leverage depends on who can redirect, wait, or reverse.
antagonists: The raw antagonist pressure is strongest on confirmation-bias and opportunity-cost, but only current-vocabulary ids with clean wording should survive Wave 3.
structured_tensions: The source names clear conflict with confirmation-bias and direct synergy with lock-in, which makes conservative first-order relation retention possible.
